event: Mesolithic hunter-gatherers establish the earliest known human presence on the island, leaving flint tools at sites such as Glen Wyllin and Cass ny Hawin.
Neolithic farmers settle the island, building megalithic tombs such as Cashtal yn Ard and King Orry's Grave.
Bronze Age communities raise burial cairns and standing stones, including the ritual monuments at Meayll Hill.
Iron Age Celtic culture takes hold, with promontory forts and roundhouses such as those at Cronk ny Merriu and South Barrule.
Goidelic-speaking Celts from Ireland introduce the Gaelic language and culture that becomes the basis of Manx.
Early Christianity reaches the island, marked by numerous small chapels (keeills) and inscribed memorial stones.
Viking raiders begin attacking the island, beginning the era of Norse incursions into the Irish Sea.
Norse settlers colonise the island, blending with the Gaelic population and leaving runic crosses and place-names.
Tynwald, the island's parliament, is traditionally founded by the Norse, making it one of the oldest continuous parliaments in the world.
Godred Crovan defeats the islanders at the Battle of Skyhill and establishes a dynasty ruling the Kingdom of Mann and the Isles.
Rushen Abbey is founded by Cistercian monks, becoming a major centre of religion and learning on the island.
Somerled defeats Godred II at sea, splitting the Kingdom of Mann and the Isles between Norse-Gaelic rulers.
A Norwegian fleet ravages the island during the dynastic conflicts of the Crovan kings.
Magnus Olafsson, the last Norse King of Mann, dies, ending the independent Norse kingdom.
The Treaty of Perth transfers the Isle of Man from Norway to the Kingdom of Scotland.
England seizes control of the island as Scottish and English crowns contest possession.
Robert the Bruce captures Castle Rushen and briefly restores Scottish control of the island.
Edward III grants the island to William de Montacute, beginning a period of feudal lordship under English magnates.
Henry IV grants the Isle of Man to Sir John Stanley, whose family rules as Lords of Mann for over two centuries.
John Stanley II visits the island and codifies its laws, strengthening the authority of the Lord of Mann.
Thomas Stanley assumes the title 'Lord of Mann' in place of the grander 'King of Mann' used by earlier rulers.
James Stanley, 7th Earl of Derby, asserts firm control during the English Civil War, fortifying the island for the Royalists.
Manx militia leader Illiam Dhone leads a rebellion that helps deliver the island to Parliamentary forces under the Commonwealth.
Illiam Dhone (William Christian) is executed at Hango Hill after the Restoration, becoming a Manx national hero.
The Act of Settlement secures Manx tenants' land rights, earning the name the island's 'Magna Carta'.
The lordship passes to the Dukes of Atholl through the Stanley line's failure of male heirs.
The Revestment Act transfers sovereignty of the island from the Atholl lords to the British Crown to curb smuggling.
The Atholl family's remaining manorial rights are bought out by the Crown, completing British control.
Steam packet services begin linking the island to Britain, opening it to trade and later tourism.
The House of Keys Election Act makes the lower house of Tynwald a popularly elected body, ending self-perpetuating membership.
The Isle of Man Railway opens its first line between Douglas and Peel, spurring the Victorian tourism boom.
The Isle of Man becomes the first national jurisdiction to grant women property owners the right to vote in general elections.
The Manx Electric Railway begins operating, becoming part of the island's enduring heritage transport network.
The first Isle of Man TT motorcycle races are held, establishing the island's famous Mountain Course event.
Knockaloe internment camp opens near Peel, holding tens of thousands of enemy aliens during the First World War.
Internment camps are reopened during the Second World War to hold enemy aliens, including many refugees from Nazi Germany.
Coxswain Robert Lee and a Ramsey lifeboat crew exemplify the island's maritime rescue tradition during the postwar era.
The Isle of Man Act reduces UK interference in Manx affairs, advancing the island's domestic autonomy.
Manx Radio launches as the island's national broadcaster, predating commercial radio in the UK.
The Isle of Man secures a special relationship with the European Economic Community via Protocol 3 rather than full membership.
Ned Maddrell, the last native speaker of traditional Manx Gaelic, dies, prompting major revival efforts.
The island celebrates the Tynwald Millennium, marking a thousand years of its parliament.
The Treasury establishes the island as a leading offshore financial centre, transforming its modern economy.
Judicial corporal punishment is finally abolished on the island, ending birching as a court-ordered penalty.
The Bunscoill Ghaelgagh, a Manx-language primary school, opens at St John's to nurture the revived Manx tongue.
Manx Gaelic is recognised by UNESCO's framework as a revitalising language after near-extinction.
UNESCO designates the entire Isle of Man as a Biosphere Reserve, the first whole-territory biosphere.
The island closes its borders and imposes strict controls during the COVID-19 pandemic, achieving long virus-free periods.
